content:
# Luke Chapter 10 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 99-100 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_010_page_001.jpg, LUK_010_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And after these things, the Lord appointed seventy-two others, and sent them two by two before his face into every city and place where he himself was about to come.

**2** And he was saying to them, "The harvest indeed is plentiful, but the laborers are few; therefore beseech the Lord of the harvest that he may send out laborers into his harvest.

**3** Go; behold, I send you out as lambs in the midst of wolves.

**4** Carry no purse, no bag, no sandals; and greet no one along the road.

**5** And into whatever house you enter, first say, 'Peace to this house.'

**6** And if a son of peace is there, your peace shall rest upon him; but if not, it shall return upon you.

**7** And in that same house remain, eating and drinking the things provided by them; for the laborer is worthy of his wages. Do not move from house to house.

**8** And into whatever city you enter and they receive you, eat the things set before you,

**9** and heal the sick in it, and say to them, 'The kingdom of God has drawn near to you.'

**10** But into whatever city you enter and they do not receive you, go out into its streets and say,

**11** 'Even the dust from your city that clings to our feet we wipe off against you; nevertheless, know this, that the kingdom of God has drawn near.'

**12** I say to you that it shall be more tolerable for Sodom in that day than for that city.

**13** Woe to you, Chorazin! Woe to you, Bethsaida! For if the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon which were done in you, they would have repented long ago, sitting in sackcloth and ashes.

**14** But it shall be more tolerable for Tyre and Sidon in the judgment than for you.

**15** And you, Capernaum, shall you be exalted to heaven? You shall be brought down to Hades.

**16** The one who hears you hears me, and the one who rejects you rejects me; and the one who rejects me rejects the one who sent me."

**17** And the seventy-two returned with joy, saying, "Lord, even the demons are subject to us in your name."

**18** And he said to them, "I was watching Satan, like lightning, having fallen from heaven.

**19** Behold, I have given you authority to tread upon serpents and scorpions, and over all the power of the enemy; and nothing shall by any means harm you.

**20** Nevertheless, do not rejoice in this, that the spirits are subject to you; but rejoice that your names have been written in the heavens."

**21** In that same hour he rejoiced in the Holy Spirit and said, "I praise you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, that you have hidden these things from the wise and understanding, and have revealed them to infants. Yes, Father, for thus it was well-pleasing before you.

**22** All things have been delivered to me by my Father; and no one knows who the Son is except the Father, and who the Father is except the Son, and he to whom the Son wills to reveal him."

**23** And having turned to the disciples, he said privately, "Blessed are the eyes that see what you see.

**24** For I say to you that many prophets and kings desired to see what you see and did not see it, and to hear what you hear and did not hear it."

**25** And behold, a certain lawyer stood up, testing him, saying, "Teacher, what shall I do to inherit eternal life?"

**26** And he said to him, "What has been written in the law? How do you read it?"

**27** And he answered and said, "You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your strength and with all your mind, and your neighbor as yourself."

**28** And he said to him, "You have answered rightly; do this, and you shall live."

**29** But he, wishing to justify himself, said to Jesus, "And who is my neighbor?"

**30** And Jesus, taking up the question, said, "A certain man was going down from Jerusalem to Jericho, and he fell among robbers, who, having both stripped him and inflicted wounds, departed, leaving him half dead.

**31** And by coincidence, a certain priest was going down on that road, and having seen him, he passed by on the other side.

**32** And likewise also a Levite, having come upon the place and having seen him, passed by on the other side.

**33** But a certain Samaritan, journeying, came upon him, and having seen him, was moved with compassion.

**34** And having come to him, he bound up his wounds, pouring on oil and wine; and having set him upon his own beast, he brought him to an inn and took care of him.

**35** And on the next day, having taken out two denarii, he gave them to the innkeeper and said, 'Take care of him, and whatever more you spend, I, when I return, will repay you.'

**36** Which of these three do you think became a neighbor to the one who fell among the robbers?"

**37** And he said, "The one who showed mercy to him." And Jesus said to him, "Go, and you do likewise."

**38** And it came to pass, as they were journeying, that he entered into a certain village; and a certain woman named Martha received him into her house.

**39** And she had a sister called Mary, who also, having sat at the feet of the Lord, was listening to his word.

**40** But Martha was distracted with much serving, and having come to him, she said, "Lord, does it not concern you that my sister has left me to serve alone? Tell her therefore to help me."

**41** But the Lord answered and said to her, "Martha, Martha, you are anxious and troubled about many things;

**42** but one thing is needful. And Mary has chosen the good portion, which shall not be taken away from her."

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Manuscript Verification

The two Sinaiticus manuscript images (pages 99-100) were examined. Page 1 covers approximately Luke 9:51 through Luke 10:13 across four columns. Page 2 covers approximately Luke 10:21 through Luke 10:42 across four columns. The text is in Sinaiticus's characteristic continuous uncial script, four columns per page. Key readings confirmed from the manuscript:

- ΕΒΔΟΜΗΚΟΝΤΑ ΔΥΟ (10:1, "seventy-two" — the Alexandrian reading, supported by Vaticanus and P75)
- ΑΝΕΔΕΙΞΕΝ (10:1, "appointed/publicly designated" — a formal commissioning verb)
- ΕΤΕΡΟΥΣ (10:1, "others" — distinguishing this group from the Twelve)
- ΜΗ ΥΠΟΔΗΜΑΤΑ (10:4, "no sandals" — urgency of the mission)
- ΥΙΟΣ ΕΙΡΗΝΗΣ (10:6, "son of peace" — Semitic idiom retained)
- ΤΟΝ ΚΟΛΛΗΘΕΝΤΑ (10:11, "having clung" — vivid participle for dust)
- ΜΗ ΕΩΣ ΟΥΡΑΝΟΥ ΥΨΩΘΗΣΗ (10:15, rhetorical question expecting negative answer)
- ΕΒΔΟΜΗΚΟΝΤΑ ΔΥΟ (10:17, "seventy-two" — consistent with verse 1)
- ΕΘΕΩΡΟΥΝ (10:18, "I was watching" — imperfect tense, continuous observation)
- ΠΕΣΟΝΤΑ (10:18, "having fallen" — aorist participle, completed fall)
- ΔΕΔΩΚΑ (10:19, "I have given" — perfect tense, enduring authority)
- ΗΓΑΛΛΙΑΣΑΤΟ ΤΩ ΠΝΕΥΜΑΤΙ ΤΩ ΑΓΙΩ (10:21, "rejoiced in the Holy Spirit" — double article)
- ΕΞΟΜΟΛΟΓΟΥΜΑΙ (10:21, "I praise/confess" — dual sense of acknowledgment and adoration)
- ΝΟΜΙΚΟΣ (10:25, "lawyer" — Lukan term for Torah expert)
- ΚΑΤΑ ΣΥΓΚΥΡΙΑΝ (10:31, "by coincidence" — NT hapax legomenon)
- ΕΣΠΛΑΓΧΝΙΣΘΗ (10:33, "was moved with compassion" — visceral, gut-level mercy)
- ΚΥΡΙΟΥ (10:39, "Lord" — not "Jesus" as in some witnesses)
- ΕΝΟΣ ΔΕ ΕΣΤΙΝ ΧΡΕΙΑ (10:42, "one thing is needful" — shorter Sinaiticus reading)

### Translation Principles Applied

1. **10:2 — "plentiful" vs. "much":** EMET renders "much" (literal for polys), LOGOS "plentiful," PNEUMA "plentiful." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "plentiful" is the natural English collocate for "harvest."

2. **10:2 — "laborers" vs. "workers":** EMET renders "workers," LOGOS "laborers," PNEUMA "workers." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "laborers" has the more traditional register appropriate to harvest imagery and echoes established biblical English.

3. **10:2 — "beseech" vs. "ask":** EMET renders "ask," LOGOS "beseech," PNEUMA "Ask." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "beseech" captures the urgency of deethete (aorist passive imperative of deomai), which is stronger than a simple request.

4. **10:4 — "road" vs. "way":** EMET renders "way," LOGOS "road," PNEUMA "road." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "road" is more concrete for hodos in this travel context.

5. **10:7 — "provided by them" vs. "from them":** EMET renders "the things from them," LOGOS "the things provided by them," PNEUMA "whatever they provide." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "provided by them" clarifies the elliptical Greek (ta par auton) without departing from the meaning.

6. **10:11 — Dust clause structure:** EMET follows the Greek word order closely ("the dust having clung to us from your city to our feet"), LOGOS rearranges for clarity ("the dust from your city that clings to our feet"), PNEUMA simplifies ("the dust of your town that clings to our feet"). SOPHIA follows LOGOS — the rearrangement preserves the vivid image while making the English readable.

7. **10:13 — "Woe" vs. "How terrible":** EMET and LOGOS render "Woe to you," PNEUMA renders "How terrible it will be for you." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "Woe" is the established translation for ouai and preserves the prophetic register.

8. **10:15 — "Hades" vs. "realm of the dead":** EMET and LOGOS retain "Hades," PNEUMA renders "the realm of the dead." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "Hades" is the proper noun in the Greek and should be transliterated, consistent with SOPHIA practice.

9. **10:18 — Vision of Satan's fall:** EMET renders "I was watching Satan, as lightning, having fallen out of the heaven," LOGOS "I was beholding Satan having fallen like lightning from heaven," PNEUMA "I watched Satan fall from heaven like a flash of lightning." SOPHIA renders "I was watching Satan, like lightning, having fallen from heaven" — preserving the imperfect etheorown ("was watching"), the aorist participle pesonta ("having fallen" — a completed fall, not an ongoing process), and "like lightning" for the comparison. "Out of" is simplified to "from."

10. **10:21 — "infants" vs. "babes" vs. "little children":** EMET renders "infants," LOGOS "babes," PNEUMA "those as open as little children." SOPHIA follows EMET — "infants" is the most precise rendering of nepiois; LOGOS's "babes" is archaic, and PNEUMA adds interpretive expansion.

11. **10:21 — "I praise you" vs. "I confess-praise to you":** EMET renders "I confess-praise to you" (compound gloss), LOGOS "I praise you," PNEUMA "I praise you." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "I praise you" renders exomologoumai effectively; the dual sense is real but the hyphenated compound is not natural English.

12. **10:25 — "lawyer" vs. "law-expert" vs. "legal expert":** EMET renders "law-expert," LOGOS "lawyer," PNEUMA "legal expert." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "lawyer" is the established formal equivalent for nomikos; EMET's hyphenation and PNEUMA's two-word phrase are unnecessary.

13. **10:25 — Question form:** EMET renders "having done what, shall I inherit eternal life?" LOGOS "what shall I do to inherit eternal life?" PNEUMA "what must I do to inherit eternal life?" SOPHIA follows LOGOS — natural English word order while preserving the interrogative force.

14. **10:27 — Preposition for "heart":** EMET renders "out of your whole heart" (literal for ex holes), LOGOS and PNEUMA render "with all your heart." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "with all your heart" is the established rendering of the Shema in English; the shift from ex to en in the Greek is a subtlety best noted rather than forced into English.

15. **10:30 — "taking up the question" vs. "having taken this up":** EMET renders "having taken this up," LOGOS "taking up the question," PNEUMA "In reply." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "taking up the question" explains the nuanced verb hypolabon clearly.

16. **10:30 — "inflicted wounds" vs. "laid on blows":** EMET renders "having laid on blows," LOGOS "inflicted wounds," PNEUMA "beat him." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "inflicted wounds" is precise for the Greek plēgas epithentes without being overly literal.

17. **10:33 — "journeying" vs. "traveling":** EMET renders "traveling," LOGOS "journeying," PNEUMA "as he traveled." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "journeying" is the more formal register appropriate to parabolic narrative.

18. **10:34 — "beast" vs. "animal" vs. "donkey":** EMET renders "his own animal," LOGOS "his own beast," PNEUMA "his own donkey." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "beast" is the traditional rendering for ktenos (a generic beast of burden); PNEUMA's "donkey" specifies beyond the Greek.

19. **10:35 — "on the next day" vs. "on the morrow":** EMET renders "on the next day," LOGOS "on the morrow," PNEUMA "the next day." SOPHIA renders "on the next day" — following EMET's phrasing which is clear without the archaism of "morrow" (LOGOS) while retaining the prepositional structure.

20. **10:36 — "do you think" vs. "seems to you":** EMET renders "seems to you to have become," LOGOS "do you think became," PNEUMA "do you think became." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "do you think" renders dokei soi naturally.

21. **10:37 — "showed mercy to him" vs. "having done the mercy with him":** EMET renders "the one having done the mercy with him," LOGOS "the one who showed mercy to him," PNEUMA "the one who showed him mercy." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "showed mercy to him" is clear English for the Greek poiesas to eleos met autou.

22. **10:37 — Final command:** EMET renders "Go, and you yourself do likewise," LOGOS "Go, and you do likewise," PNEUMA "Go and do the same." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "Go, and you do likewise" preserves the emphatic su ("you") and the traditional "likewise" for homoios.

23. **10:39 — "was listening" vs. "was hearing":** EMET renders "was hearing his word," LOGOS "was listening to his word," PNEUMA "listened to what he was saying." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "was listening" (imperfect) conveys attentive, ongoing reception; EMET's "hearing" is weaker in English.

24. **10:40 — "distracted with much serving" vs. "distracted about much serving":** EMET renders "distracted about much serving," LOGOS "distracted with much serving," PNEUMA "pulled in every direction by all the preparations." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "distracted with" is the natural English preposition; PNEUMA is too expansive.

25. **10:40 — "having come to him" vs. "having stood over him":** EMET renders "having stood over him" (literal for epistasa), LOGOS "having come to him," PNEUMA "came to him." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — epistasa can mean "standing by/approaching"; "having come to him" is clear without the confrontational overtone of "stood over."

26. **10:41 — "But the Lord answered" vs. "And having answered, the Lord":** EMET renders "And having answered, the Lord said to her," LOGOS "But the Lord answered and said to her." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — the adversative "But" better captures the contrast between Martha's complaint and Jesus' response.

27. **10:42 — "And Mary has chosen" vs. "For Mary chose":** EMET renders "For Mary chose the good part," LOGOS "And Mary has chosen the good portion," PNEUMA "Mary has chosen the better part." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "the good portion" preserves the Greek agathen merida precisely; PNEUMA's "better" (comparative) exceeds the Greek positive agathen. LOGOS's "And" correctly renders the connective.

---

## Disagreement Log

| Verse | Issue | EMET (Literal) | LOGOS (Formal) | PNEUMA (Dynamic) | SOPHIA Decision | Rationale |
|-------|-------|----------------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|-----------|
| 10:2 | "plentiful" vs. "much" | "much" | "plentiful" | "plentiful" | "plentiful" | 2-to-1; natural collocate for "harvest" |
| 10:2 | "laborers" vs. "workers" | "workers" | "laborers" | "workers" | "laborers" | Traditional register for harvest imagery; *ergatai* |
| 10:2 | "beseech" vs. "ask" | "ask" | "beseech" | "Ask" | "beseech" | *deomai* is stronger than simple request; urgency preserved |
| 10:4 | "road" vs. "way" | "way" | "road" | "road" | "road" | 2-to-1; more concrete for *hodos* in travel context |
| 10:6 | "is there" vs. "be there" vs. "lives there" | "is there" | "be there" | "lives there" | "is there" | EMET and LOGOS close; "is" is simpler and accurate |
| 10:7 | "from them" vs. "provided by them" | "the things from them" | "the things provided by them" | "whatever they provide" | "the things provided by them" | LOGOS clarifies elliptical *ta par auton* naturally |
| 10:10 | "having gone out" vs. "go out" | "having gone out... say" | "go out... and say" | "go out... and say" | "go out... and say" | 2-to-1; imperative flow is clearer |
| 10:11 | Dust clause structure | Greek word order preserved | Rearranged for clarity | Simplified | LOGOS rearrangement | Readable English preserving the vivid image |
| 10:13 | "Woe" vs. "How terrible" | "Woe to you" | "Woe to you" | "How terrible it will be for you" | "Woe to you" | 2-to-1; prophetic register preserved for *ouai* |
| 10:13 | "mighty works" vs. "miracles" | "mighty-works" | "mighty works" | "miracles" | "mighty works" | 2-to-1; *dynameis* = acts of power, not simply "miracles" |
| 10:15 | "Hades" vs. "realm of the dead" | "Hades" | "Hades" | "the realm of the dead" | "Hades" | 2-to-1; transliterate proper noun consistently |
| 10:18 | Satan's fall rendering | "as lightning, having fallen out of the heaven" | "having fallen like lightning from heaven" | "fall from heaven like a flash of lightning" | "like lightning, having fallen from heaven" | Preserves aorist *pesonta* (completed fall); "from" for "out of" |
| 10:18 | "I was watching" vs. "I was beholding" | "I was watching" | "I was beholding" | "I watched" | "I was watching" | EMET and LOGOS preserve imperfect; "watching" is less archaic than "beholding" |
| 10:21 | "infants" vs. "babes" | "infants" | "babes" | "those as open as little children" | "infants" | Most precise for *nepiois*; "babes" archaic; PNEUMA expands |
| 10:21 | "I praise" vs. "I confess-praise" | "I confess-praise to you" | "I praise you" | "I praise you" | "I praise you" | 2-to-1; hyphenated compound is not natural English |
| 10:25 | "lawyer" vs. "law-expert" | "law-expert" | "lawyer" | "legal expert" | "lawyer" | Established formal equivalent for *nomikos* |
| 10:25 | Question form | "having done what, shall I inherit" | "what shall I do to inherit" | "what must I do to inherit" | "what shall I do to inherit" | Natural English word order; LOGOS faithful |
| 10:27 | Preposition for "heart" | "out of your whole heart" | "with all your heart" | "with all your heart" | "with all your heart" | 2-to-1; established Shema rendering in English |
| 10:30 | *hypolabon* rendering | "having taken this up" | "taking up the question" | "In reply" | "taking up the question" | LOGOS explains nuanced verb clearly |
| 10:30 | "inflicted wounds" vs. "laid on blows" | "having laid on blows" | "inflicted wounds" | "beat him" | "inflicted wounds" | Precise for *plēgas epithentes*; PNEUMA too simple |
| 10:33 | "journeying" vs. "traveling" | "traveling" | "journeying" | "as he traveled" | "journeying" | More formal register for parabolic narrative |
| 10:33 | "was moved with compassion" vs. "his heart went out" | "was moved-with-compassion" | "was moved with compassion" | "his heart went out to him" | "was moved with compassion" | 2-to-1; PNEUMA is interpretive paraphrase of *esplagchnisthe* |
| 10:34 | "beast" vs. "animal" vs. "donkey" | "his own animal" | "his own beast" | "his own donkey" | "his own beast" | Traditional rendering; *ktenos* is generic; PNEUMA specifies beyond Greek |
| 10:35 | "on the next day" vs. "on the morrow" | "on the next day" | "on the morrow" | "the next day" | "on the next day" | Clear without archaism; preserves prepositional structure |
| 10:36 | "seems to you" vs. "do you think" | "seems to you" | "do you think" | "do you think" | "do you think" | 2-to-1; natural English for *dokei soi* |
| 10:37 | Mercy description | "the one having done the mercy with him" | "the one who showed mercy to him" | "the one who showed him mercy" | "the one who showed mercy to him" | LOGOS is clear English for *poiesas to eleos met autou* |
| 10:37 | Final command | "Go, and you yourself do likewise" | "Go, and you do likewise" | "Go and do the same" | "Go, and you do likewise" | Preserves emphatic *su* and traditional "likewise" |
| 10:39 | "hearing" vs. "listening" | "was hearing his word" | "was listening to his word" | "listened to what he was saying" | "was listening to his word" | "Listening" conveys attentive reception; imperfect preserved |
| 10:40 | Distraction phrase | "distracted about much serving" | "distracted with much serving" | "pulled in every direction" | "distracted with much serving" | Natural English preposition; PNEUMA too expansive |
| 10:40 | Martha's approach | "having stood over him" | "having come to him" | "came to him" | "having come to him" | *epistasa* can mean approach; avoids confrontational overtone |
| 10:41 | Introductory conjunction | "And having answered" | "But the Lord answered" | "But the Lord answered" | "But the Lord answered" | 2-to-1; adversative "But" captures the contrast |
| 10:42 | "good portion" vs. "good part" vs. "better part" | "the good part" | "the good portion" | "the better part" | "the good portion" | *merida* = "portion"; PNEUMA's "better" exceeds positive *agathen* |

---

*Compiled by SOPHIA in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA, from Codex Sinaiticus (4th century CE).*
